replacement_html:
6.13 The MOND Grouping — A Category-Error Import

Five of the eleven WINs added in late March 2026 (WIN-047, 048, 052, 054, 055) trace item-for-item to the modified-gravity / MOND community's canonical anomaly list — the Radial Acceleration Relation, El Gordo, the CMB Axis of Evil, Hubble tension, Cepheid tension. The remaining six (tidal constituents WIN-045/046/049/050/051 and dome-internal derivations WIN-044/053) fall under adjacent but distinct patterns: standard physics relabeled as dome predictions, and dome-internal failed derivations. Independent convergence on this same list would be striking corroboration. Importing it without a single derivation from disc-cosmology principles is not corroboration — it is borrowing, and the dome's own bounded-firmament commitments (disc radius ~20,015 km; firmament height ~9,086 km) make the derivation route formally impossible at the first dimensional step (data/wins.json:WIN-047,048,052,054,055; dome coordinates.html).

The kernel — the dome's own taxonomy already had a coherent home

Before attacking the import, acknowledge what the dome author got right: his own published claim taxonomy already contained a coherent sub-category for arguments of the form "the standard model has trouble explaining X." The WIN-048 prose for the CMB Axis of Evil is explicitly labeled by the dome as a "ΛCDM Counter-Prediction" — a sub-framing that admits the rhetorical structure is "the alternative is in trouble," not "my model is right" (data/wins.json:WIN-048 detail_evidence). Applied consistently to the other four cosmological items in the same March 2026 commit burst (WIN-047 Hubble, WIN-052 RAR, WIN-054 El Gordo, WIN-055 Cepheid), that label would have produced a defensible — if much weaker — claim structure. The author had the right concept available. The category-error here is not that the items are wrong to surface; it is that they are surfaced under the strongest-claim category (confirmed WINs, tallied into the headline "69 Confirmed Predictions" scorecard on the home page) when the author's own taxonomy contained a more accurate one. That placement is where the self-own enters, and the closed-world commitments documented in the next two subsections then close any retreat from it.

The list, and why list-match matters

The eleven WINs added between WIN-044 and WIN-054 in three commits on the morning of 2026-03-23 (04:57, 05:14, and 05:29 UTC — 32 minutes from first to last) are not a heterogeneous selection of dome-cosmology insights that happen to coincide with MOND-community talking points. Five are direct entries from the MOND community’s canonical anomaly list: the Radial Acceleration Relation (WIN-052, citing the Mistele 2024 lensing extension at ApJL 969 L3), the El Gordo cluster framed as a “6.2σ ΛCDM impossibility” (WIN-054, the Asencio, Banik & Kroupa 2023 framing), the CMB Axis of Evil (WIN-048), Hubble tension (WIN-047), and Cepheid distance-ladder tension (WIN-055). The remaining six belong to adjacent but distinct categories: tidal constituents M2/S2/K1/O1/N2 (WIN-045/046/049/050/051) are well-explained standard geophysical phenomena not addressed by MOND; a custom-derived firmament scaling function (WIN-044) and two-pole geomagnetic model (WIN-053) are dome-internal derivations whose refutations rest on their own terms. The list is not original to the dome author. It is a published artifact of the modified-gravity research community — Stacy McGaugh maintains it in varying forms on his Triton Station blog; Pavel Kroupa and Indranil Banik in the Bonn group publish its components in their cosmology papers (see Banik & Zhao 2022, Symmetry 14(7):1331 for the canonical survey of MOND-supporting anomalies); it circulates in the MOND-curious sphere as the canonical set of anomalies where modified gravity claims its strongest empirical foothold.

The distinction between independent prediction and external import is the deciding question for whether such a list match constitutes evidence. Imagine, hypothetically, that the dome cosmology had been developed in isolation from MOND, working purely from disc-cosmology first principles, and had derived as predictions: a tight relationship between observed and baryonic acceleration in galactic systems; an inability of standard mass-function reasoning to explain a specific cluster mass at a specific redshift; an alignment of CMB low-multipole moments with a special structural axis; a discrepancy between local and CMB-derived expansion measurements. If the resulting dome predictions matched the MOND-curated list, that would be one of the strongest possible forms of corroboration — two independent theoretical programs, working from different principles, converging on the same empirical anomalies. That structure of agreement is what working scientists treat as serious evidence, and it is the kind of move that has historically resolved scientific disputes.

That is not what happened. The dome author did not derive these anomalies from disc-cosmology principles. He imported them, in a single late-March commit sequence, and labeled each one "compatible with ECM" or "consistent with disc cosmology" without specifying any dome-internal mechanism that produces them. There is no derivation chain from the disc radius to RAR's a₀ threshold. There is no dome-mechanical argument for why the El Gordo cluster's mass anomaly should be 6.2σ rather than 4σ or 8σ. There is no firmament-geometric reason the CMB should display a quadrupole-octupole alignment. The compatibility claims are textually identical in structure: "phenomenon X exists, the standard model has trouble with X, the dome model is consistent with X." That is not convergence. It is borrowing.

Why the closed-world commitment forecloses the derivation route

The dome model could in principle attempt the derivation work, and a sympathetic reading would expect it to try. The work has not been attempted, and there is a structural reason: it cannot succeed.

MOND, taken on its own merits, lives at galactic and cosmological scales. The critical acceleration a₀ ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ m/s² becomes relevant only at distances of order 10²⁰ meters between the observer and the dominant gravitating mass. The Radial Acceleration Relation is a relationship measured at points along galactic rotation curves, where the radius is tens of kiloparsecs and the relevant baryonic mass distribution is three-dimensional (McGaugh, Lelli & Schombert 2016). The El Gordo cluster's mass is reconstructed from gravitational lensing of background galaxies at z > 1, distances of order 10²⁵ meters. The CMB Axis of Evil is a feature of a microwave background that spans the observable universe.

The dome cosmology, by its own published parameters, has a maximum spatial extent on the order of 10⁷ meters. The disc radius is approximately 20,015 km. The firmament height function H(r) has a published apex value of approximately 9,086 km (the dome's exponential decay length constant λ = 8,619 km is the scaling parameter from WIN-053, not the rim-height value), with the dome’s own published documents listing three inconsistent peak values — 8,537, 9,086, and 9,572 km — across model.html, coordinates.html, and the predictions registry (data/wins.json:WIN-029 documents this self-contradiction; dome coordinates.html). The local sun is approximately 5,733 km away. The local moon is similarly close. There is no point within the firmament at which gravitational acceleration drops anywhere near a₀, because there are no masses distributed over the spatial extents that would produce such an acceleration profile. There is no measured rotation curve to test RAR against, because stars in the dome model are pinned to the firmament's structure rather than orbiting in galactic potentials. There are no galaxy clusters to lens with, because there are no galaxies to cluster. There is no microwave background, because there is no big bang.

The square-cube scaling problem is the most specific way to see the dimensional incompatibility. Mass distributions in MOND-relevant systems follow volume — the baryonic mass enclosed within radius R is ∫ρ(r) 4πr² dr, the integral of a three-dimensional density profile over a spherical volume. Gravitational acceleration at radius R from such a 3D mass distribution scales as GM(<R)/R². If "galaxies" in the dome model collapse to points or near-points on the two-dimensional firmament, the integrals collapse with them: there is no volume over which to integrate density, no three-dimensional mass profile to compute, no radius dependence for the enclosed-mass function. RAR's predicted relationship between observed and baryonic acceleration becomes formally undefined. The dimensional ratios that distinguish MOND from Newton — accelerations at specific radii produced by specific 3D mass profiles — have no analogue in a model where galaxies are firmament artifacts. Even if a sympathetic dome theorist attempted the derivation, the calculations would fail at the first dimensional step. The required mass distribution does not exist in the model.

The dome model has, in published form, two options for what galaxies are. Either they are real distant gravitating mass-collections — in which case the firmament is not bounded, and the dome model is something other than the dome model — or they are firmament artifacts at sub-30,000-kilometer distances — in which case mainstream measurements of galactic phenomena cannot be marshaled as evidence for the dome, because the measurements were of phenomena the dome cosmology denies exist. The MOND-grouping WINs implicitly take the first option, citing galactic anomalies as real and measured. The dome's bounded-firmament commitments require the second option. The two cannot both be held — which is the structure of self-contradicted, not merely misleading. That is the basis on which WIN-047, WIN-048, WIN-052, WIN-054, and WIN-055 are reclassified to Self-Contradicted under sub-rationale "category-error import (MOND grouping)."

A third reading the dome could attempt — galaxies as 2D illusory patterns on the firmament whose photon properties nevertheless fall along an RAR-shaped curve when measured — does not escape the contradiction. In that reading, the measured Radial Acceleration Relation is a property of the illusion-generation mechanism, not of any 3D mass distribution; the same applies to El Gordo's lensing-reconstructed cluster mass at z ≈ 0.87, the Cepheid period-luminosity ladder, and the CMB quadrupole-octupole alignment — each measurement probes whatever rules the firmament-projection runs by, not the galactic-scale 3D physics that MOND, NFW mass profiles, and standard cosmology actually invoke. The per-WIN treatment states this dilemma directly (data/wins.json:WIN-052 detail_extra): option (A) the objects exist at the claimed distances, in which case the firmament is falsified by direct observation; option (B) the objects are illusions whose measured properties cannot serve as evidence for the dome. The third-option retreat does not produce an option (C); it produces a more sophisticated version of option (B) — the dome still cannot cite the measurements as confirmations of a cosmology whose explanatory mechanism (galactic-scale 3D physics) it has now denied operates.

The category placement, and the self-own

The dome author's published claim taxonomy has, broadly, three categories visible to readers: WINs (entries in the wins registry, framed as "confirmed predictions"), Predictions / Tracking (entries in the prospective bucket awaiting test-window closure), and Kill-Shot Tests (six side-by-side discriminating tests on killshot.html where the dome and globe make different numerical predictions and one of them matches data). The Kill-Shot tab is structured for genuine binary discriminators — Sydney–Perth rail distance, Polaris elevation at 35.9°N, the August 2026 eclipse magnetic prediction at Ebro, NMP drift rate, SAA decay rate, JFK–LHR aetheric slipstream — each row pairs a dome number with a globe number and a measured outcome. It is not a tab for "things mainstream cosmology can't explain"; it requires a dome-side numerical prediction in the first place.

The dome author does have an internal sub-category for items where the argument is "the standard model has trouble with this." The WIN-048 prose for the CMB Axis of Evil is explicitly labeled by the dome as a "ΛCDM Counter-Prediction" — the dome's own framing acknowledges that the argument structure is not "the dome predicts X" but "ΛCDM cannot explain X" (data/wins.json:WIN-048 detail_evidence). This sub-category exists. It lives inside the WINs registry rather than in a separate tab, but the rhetorical distinction is one the dome author understands well enough to label.

The self-own is in the placement decision. The MOND-grouping items are flagged as confirmed WINs — entries in the registry tallied as evidence for the dome cosmology, contributing to the headline "69 Confirmed Predictions" scorecard on the home page. Filing WIN-052 as a confirmed WIN means asserting that disc cosmology is confirmed by the Radial Acceleration Relation. Filing WIN-054 as a confirmed WIN means asserting it is confirmed by the El Gordo cluster's mass anomaly. The dome's own "ΛCDM Counter-Prediction" sub-framing — at least internally consistent in admitting the argument is "the alternative is in trouble, not my model is right" — was available and was sometimes used (WIN-048 carries the label). It was not used for the rest of the grouping. The author chose the strongest available claim category for material that does not warrant it. That choice, given what was imported, makes the placement itself incoherent: confirmation requires that the dome model contain the phenomenon in question and produce the observed value. Under the dome's own bounded-firmament commitments, none of the five reclassified WINs meet that requirement, and the dome's own sub-category framing already concedes as much for at least one of them.

The deeper lesson

The MOND grouping illustrates a general structural problem with the dome project's approach to scientific corroboration. The author appears to have assumed that any anti-mainstream finding within physics counts as friendly to disc cosmology, because both are in tension with the standard model. The assumption is invalid. The fringe of real science is still real science. It carries empirical commitments. MOND commits to galactic-scale 3D physics. Plasma cosmology commits to electromagnetic structure across cosmic distances. Modified-inertia models commit to their own dimensional architectures. Each fringe research program operates within a framework with specific assumptions that are independently measurable and independently constraining. None of those frameworks accommodate a closed-world firmament cosmology bounded at thirty thousand kilometers. Each, in its own way, requires the universe to be much larger than the dome allows.

This is the deeper observation. The MOND community's anomaly list is not the only place where the dome author could have looked for fringe-but-real scientific support, and the choice he made is in some sense the worst available — not because MOND is bad science (it is not; it is among the most carefully derived of the modified-gravity programs), but because MOND's empirical legitimacy comes precisely from frameworks that operate at scales where galaxies, clusters, the CMB, and cosmological expansion are real entities producing real measurements. The legitimacy that made MOND attractive to import is the same legitimacy that makes the import a self-defeating move. Real-science legitimacy comes from frameworks that operate at scales where the dome cosmology cannot exist, and the dome model cannot have its cosmology be smaller than its sources of legitimacy.

The MOND grouping is not evidence for the dome cosmology. It is the clearest available demonstration that the dome cosmology is incompatible with the empirical commitments of every nearby program in the modified-gravity / alternative-cosmology landscape. The dome author was searching among the most legitimate fringe science available, hoping to find his cosmology's neighbors. The neighborhood does not exist.

new_evidence_added: WIN-048 detail_evidence — already cited in the original prose; this restructure leads with it rather than burying it (data/wins.json:WIN-048 detail_evidence).

Cross-reference to the four other cosmological WINs (047/052/054/055) where the same sub-label could have been applied — already cited at the section level; this restructure surfaces the missed-opportunity framing explicitly.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding a kernel-of-truth h3 at the top inflates the section by ~270 words. Part 6 subsections are already long.
anticipated_objections.response: Net inflation is one paragraph (~247 words including the h3). Comparator p6-614-kappa-cluster opens with a similarly-sized "what κ is and why the dome cannot abandon it" h3 and runs to ~18 KB total. SEC-6.13 post-insertion is ~16.1 KB, still inside the Part 6 size band (3.3 KB to 22.8 KB).

anticipated_objections.objection: The kernel-of-truth content duplicates material in the existing "The category placement, and the self-own" h3.
anticipated_objections.response: Partial overlap is intentional and matches Part 6 pattern: lead with the dome's own taxonomy choice (kernel framing — "the author had the right concept available"), then later subsection drives home the placement-as-WIN consequence in detail. The lead does the rhetorical setup; the later subsection does the empirical heavy lifting. p6-614-kappa-cluster has the same structure (κ-as-dome-load-bearing in the first h3, κ-predicts-zero-but-saw-zero in the later h3s).

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not just rewrite "The category placement, and the self-own" h3 to lead with the kernel? That would avoid the redundancy.
anticipated_objections.response: Moving the self-own h3 to the top means rebuilding the section's argumentative arc (currently: list → closed-world → placement → deeper lesson). Inserting a short kernel h3 at the top preserves that arc and adds the missing rhetorical setup. Lower-risk and matches what the curmudgeon explicitly recommended in h5 option (a).

anticipated_objections.objection: The curmudgeon offered "leave as-is" as option (b). That was cheaper.
anticipated_objections.response: The decider chose to route ISS-2115 to analyst (not close as wontfix), with class_hint=deep-attack and severity=moderate. That signals the kernel-led framing is wanted. Curmudgeon's option (a) is the natural fulfillment.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author had the right rhetorical concept available — his own "ΛCDM Counter-Prediction" sub-label on WIN-048 is internally consistent in admitting the argument is "the alternative is in trouble," not "my model is right." Applied consistently across the cosmological items in the March 2026 commit burst, that sub-label would have produced a coherent (if much weaker) claim structure. This restructure leads with the author's correct concept before showing why the placement-as-WIN turns it into a self-contradiction.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: The kernel is rhetorical, not structural. Having the right sub-label available does not change the closed-world dimensional argument: the dome's ~10^7 m extent is geometrically incompatible with the galactic / cosmological-scale physics the imported anomalies are about, regardless of which taxonomy slot the dome author files them under. The kernel illuminates why this is a self-own (the author had the better option and chose worse) rather than merely a mistake, but it does not rescue the cosmological WINs from Self-Contradicted status.

size_compliance.part6_size_band: yes — falls in the 3.3 KB to 22.8 KB Part 6 range; insertion adds ~270 bytes total.

progressive_disclosure_compliance.note: Insertion is content-only inside the existing `<div class="ps-detail">` wrapper. No wrapper-level changes.

summary_for_decider: Single-paragraph kernel-of-truth h3 insertion at top of p6-613-mond-grouping. Closes curmudgeon hole h5 (moderate). review_class: deep-attack matches class_hint from decider. Replacement HTML is the FULL new subsection (TLDR unchanged + new h3 + all existing h3s unchanged), so decider can swap by find/replace on the existing <details id="p6-613-mond-grouping">...</details> block in data/sections.json:part6.html. No other holes from SEC-6.13-mond-grouping.json are addressed by this EXP — h1/h2/h3/h4/h6/h7/h8/h9 remain open if any have separate ISSs (cross-check before closing the curmudgeon review).
